title: OOP vs Procedural: Identify the Style
prompt_md:
## Goal
Read a description code pattern and print whether it demonstrates OOP style or procedural style, then print the reason.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 4), the number of scenarios.
- Lines 2..N+1: each line is one of the following codes:
  - `A` — A struct whose behavior lives in standalone functions that take the struct by reference.
  - `B` — A class where functions are defined inside the class and operate on its own data.
  - `C` — A standalone function that computes a result from two plain integer parameters with no struct or class.
  - `D` — A class with a private data member that can only be changed through a member function.

## Output
For each scenario, print one line:
- `A: Procedural`
- `B: OOP`
- `C: Procedural`
- `D: OOP`

## Requirements
1. Print exactly the lines shown above for each input code.
2. N can be 1 to 4; handle all combinations.
3. Codes always appear in the set {A, B, C, D}.

test_cases.label: Hidden: all four codes
test_cases.expected_stdout:
A: Procedural
B: OOP
C: Procedural
D: OOP

title: Procedural to OOP Migration Bug
prompt_md:
## Goal
The program below tracks a simple score counter. It is supposed to start at zero, apply two increases, and print the final score. Find and fix the one logic bug.

## Input
No input — the program uses hard-coded values.

## Output
Print exactly one line:
```
Final score: 15
```

## Requirements
1. The program must print `Final score: 15` (capital F, colon, space, no trailing blank lines).
2. The bug is a single logic error — the program compiles as-is.
3. Do not add or remove functions; only fix the bug.
